category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Supervised learning
items.description: Supervised learning trains a model to map inputs to outputs using examples that already carry known target labels, optimizing a loss that penalizes the gap between predictions and ground truth. The trained function is then expected to generalize to unseen inputs drawn from the same distribution. Its effectiveness depends directly on the quantity, balance, and label quality of the training data.
items.applicability: Classification and regression tasks where labeled examples exist, such as document type detection, defect prediction, or credit-decision scoring.
items.example: Training a classifier on thousands of emails labeled spam or not-spam so it can flag new incoming messages.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Performance degrades when production data drifts from the labeled training distribution, and labeling errors propagate directly into the model.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Unsupervised learning
items.description: Unsupervised learning discovers structure in data that has no target labels, typically through clustering, density estimation, or dimensionality reduction. Rather than predicting a known answer, it groups similar observations or compresses features to expose latent patterns. Because there is no ground truth, evaluating the result usually requires human interpretation or downstream task performance.
items.applicability: Exploratory analysis, customer or document segmentation, anomaly detection, and feature compression where labels are unavailable or expensive.
items.example: Clustering imported source files by embedding similarity to surface natural module groupings without predefined categories.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Results are sensitive to algorithm and hyperparameter choices, and cluster meaning is interpretive rather than objectively correct.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Embeddings
items.description: An embedding maps text, code, or other items into a fixed-length numeric vector whose geometric proximity reflects semantic similarity. Models are trained so that related inputs land near each other, enabling similarity search, clustering, and retrieval over a vector space rather than exact string matching. Embedding dimensionality and the source model determine how much meaning the vector can capture.
items.applicability: Semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation, deduplication, and recommendation over text or code corpora.
items.example: LocalAIFactory encodes knowledge chunks with a 768-dimension model so a query vector can retrieve the nearest stored chunks from a vector store.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Embeddings inherit the training model's biases and domain gaps, and vectors from different models are not comparable.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
items.description: Retrieval-augmented generation grounds a language model's output by first retrieving relevant documents from an external store and injecting them into the prompt context before generation. This lets the model answer using up-to-date or private knowledge it was never trained on, and provides a basis for citing sources. Output quality is bounded by retrieval quality: if the retrieved passages are irrelevant or missing, the model falls back on parametric knowledge or fabricates.
items.applicability: Question answering, code assistance, and knowledge injection over private corpora where the model must reflect curated or proprietary information.
items.example: LocalAIFactory injects approved knowledge and code snippets retrieved by similarity into the prompt context so responses reflect the curated project memory first.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. RAG reduces but does not eliminate hallucination, and poor chunking or stale indexes silently degrade answers.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Hallucination risk in generative models
items.description: Generative language models can produce fluent, confident output that is factually wrong or unsupported, because they optimize for plausible continuations rather than verified truth. This risk is intrinsic to the architecture and cannot be fully removed, only mitigated through grounding, retrieval, constrained decoding, and human review. The danger is highest when the model is asked about specifics it lacks evidence for, such as exact figures, citations, or API signatures.
items.applicability: Any workflow that surfaces model output to users for decisions, especially in regulated banking and middleware contexts where wrong answers carry real cost.
items.example: An assistant inventing a plausible-looking but non-existent method name or configuration key that compiles in the user's mind but not in the codebase.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Mitigations lower frequency but never guarantee correctness; treat generated facts as claims to verify, not ground truth.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Model evaluation
items.description: Model evaluation measures whether a model meets its objective using held-out data and metrics aligned to the task, such as accuracy, precision/recall, F1, calibration, or task-specific human judgment. Robust evaluation separates training, validation, and test data to avoid leakage and reports uncertainty rather than a single point number. For generative systems, automated metrics are weak proxies, so human or rubric-based assessment is usually required.
items.applicability: Gating model releases, comparing candidate models, and monitoring quality over time before and after deployment.
items.example: Scoring a defect classifier on a held-out test set with precision and recall, then confirming behavior on examples representative of production traffic.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. A single aggregate metric can hide subgroup failures and data leakage; metric choice must match the real cost of errors.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Model drift
items.description: Model drift is the degradation of a deployed model's performance as the statistical properties of real-world inputs or the input-output relationship shift away from the conditions it was trained on. Data drift refers to changing input distributions, while concept drift refers to a changing relationship between inputs and the correct output. Detecting drift requires ongoing monitoring of input distributions and outcome metrics, because the model itself will not announce that it has become stale.
items.applicability: Production ML systems with long lifespans, particularly where business processes, customer behavior, or upstream data formats evolve.
items.example: A document classifier trained on last year's form layouts steadily mislabeling a redesigned form until monitoring flags rising error rates.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Drift detection thresholds are context-dependent and can lag, so periodic revalidation against fresh labels remains necessary.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Explainability of ML models
items.description: Explainability is the degree to which a human can understand why a model produced a given output, ranging from inherently interpretable models to post-hoc techniques that approximate the reasoning of opaque ones. Feature-importance and example-based explanations help surface drivers of a decision but are approximations, not the model's literal internal logic. In regulated domains, explainability supports accountability, debugging, and the right to a meaningful reason for a decision.
items.applicability: Decisions affecting customers (credit, fraud, eligibility), model debugging, and audit or governance reviews.
items.example: Presenting the top contributing features for a flagged transaction so a reviewer can judge whether the alert is justified.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Post-hoc explanations can be unstable or misleading and do not guarantee fidelity to the true decision process.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Bias in machine learning
items.description: Bias in machine learning is systematic, unfair skew in model behavior that can arise from unrepresentative training data, historical inequities encoded in labels, proxy features, or the modeling objective itself. It can disadvantage particular groups even when no protected attribute is used directly, because correlated features act as proxies. Managing bias requires explicit fairness criteria, representative data, and measurement across subgroups, since fairness definitions can conflict and cannot all be satisfied at once.
items.applicability: Any model influencing decisions about people, and any governance process that must demonstrate fair and non-discriminatory treatment.
items.example: A scoring model that systematically rates one customer segment lower because the historical training labels reflected past biased decisions.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Different fairness metrics are mathematically incompatible in general; bias mitigation involves explicit trade-offs and ongoing measurement.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Privacy in ML systems
items.description: Privacy in machine learning concerns preventing models and pipelines from exposing personal or confidential data, whether through training on sensitive records, memorization that can be extracted, or logging of inputs. Techniques such as data minimization, de-identification, access control, and privacy-preserving training reduce exposure, but each carries a utility trade-off. Local-first architectures reduce risk by keeping data on-premise rather than sending it to external services.
items.applicability: Systems processing personal, financial, or confidential records, and any deployment subject to data-protection obligations.
items.example: LocalAIFactory runs models and stores memory locally with no internet dependency, keeping sensitive banking code and data inside the controlled environment.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Privacy techniques trade off accuracy and cost, and de-identification can be reversible; this is not legal or compliance advice.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Limitations of synthetic data
items.description: Synthetic data is artificially generated to augment scarce datasets, protect privacy, or balance rare classes, but it only approximates the real distribution and can omit the rare edge cases that matter most. Models trained heavily on synthetic data risk learning the generator's artifacts rather than real-world structure, and repeatedly training on model-generated data can compound errors. Synthetic data is a supplement to, not a replacement for, validation on genuine held-out real data.
items.applicability: Data augmentation, privacy-preserving sharing, and testing when real labeled examples are scarce or sensitive.
items.example: Generating additional rare-form examples to balance a classifier, while still validating final accuracy on real production documents.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Synthetic data can encode generator bias and miss tail events; always validate against real data before trusting results.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Model governance
items.description: Model governance is the set of policies, roles, and controls that manage models across their lifecycle: documenting purpose and data, approving deployment, monitoring performance and drift, and retiring or retraining when they degrade. It establishes accountability so that someone owns each model's risks, and creates an audit trail of decisions and changes. In financial contexts, governance also covers model-risk management, validation independence, and approval lifecycles.
items.applicability: Organizations operating models in production that must satisfy internal control, audit, or regulatory expectations.
items.example: An approval lifecycle where a model and its training data are documented, independently validated, and signed off before being promoted, with monitoring afterward.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Governance frameworks vary by jurisdiction and organization; this is awareness only, not regulatory or model-risk advice.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Empirical software engineering
items.description: Empirical software engineering studies how software is actually built and maintained using observation, controlled experiments, case studies, and mining of repository data, rather than relying on intuition or anecdote. It seeks evidence about which practices improve outcomes such as quality, productivity, and maintainability. A recurring lesson is that results are highly context-dependent, so a practice that helps one team or codebase may not transfer unchanged to another.
items.applicability: Deciding which engineering practices to adopt and grounding process decisions in evidence rather than fashion.
items.example: Mining a project's commit and defect history to see whether files changed by many authors correlate with more bugs before mandating a process change.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Empirical findings have variable effect sizes and external validity; treat them as informed priors, not universal laws.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Code-review effectiveness
items.description: Code review improves quality primarily through early defect detection, knowledge sharing, and maintaining shared standards, with much of its value coming from understanding-oriented discussion rather than purely catching bugs. Evidence consistently associates smaller, focused changes and timely reviews with higher defect-finding yield, while very large reviews lose effectiveness as reviewer attention fades. The practice also spreads familiarity with the codebase, reducing single points of knowledge.
items.applicability: Any team using pull-request or change-list review to gate merges and maintain code health.
items.example: Keeping a change small enough to review carefully in one sitting, so reviewers catch a missing null check and an unclear name before merge.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Review effectiveness depends on reviewer expertise, change size, and culture; it complements but does not replace automated testing.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Test-driven development (TDD)
items.description: Test-driven development is a workflow where a failing test is written before the production code, then code is written to pass it, then both are refactored, repeating in short cycles. The discipline tends to produce more testable designs and a regression safety net, and it forces clarity about requirements before implementation. Empirical results on defect and productivity benefits are mixed and context-dependent, with much of the value attributed to writing tests at all and working in small increments.
items.applicability: Building logic-heavy components where fast feedback and a regression suite materially reduce rework.
items.example: Writing a failing unit test for a currency-rounding rule, implementing the minimal code to pass it, then refactoring the helper with the test guarding behavior.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Reported benefits vary across studies; TDD is less suited to exploratory or UI-heavy work and can be misapplied as dogma.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Continuous integration
items.description: Continuous integration is the practice of merging developers' work into a shared mainline frequently, with each integration automatically built and tested so that defects and conflicts surface quickly. Short-lived branches and a green pipeline reduce the cost and risk of large, late merges. The associated discipline of keeping the build always releasable is widely linked to faster, more reliable delivery.
items.applicability: Teams of any size sharing a codebase who want fast feedback and to avoid painful integration phases.
items.example: A pipeline that builds the solution in Release and runs the test suite on every push, blocking merges when the build or tests fail.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Benefits depend on fast, reliable tests; a slow or flaky pipeline can erode trust and is often ignored.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Technical debt
items.description: Technical debt is the implied future cost of choosing an expedient design now over a sounder but slower one, accruing interest as the shortcut makes later changes harder and riskier. Some debt is deliberate and strategic, while some is inadvertent and arises from eroding knowledge or shifting requirements. Managing it means making debt visible, prioritizing repayment by the friction it causes, and avoiding the assumption that all debt must be paid off.
items.applicability: Planning and prioritization conversations where speed-versus-quality trade-offs and maintenance backlogs must be weighed.
items.example: Documenting a quick workaround in the backlog with the conditions under which it should be refactored before it spreads through the module.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. The debt metaphor is qualitative; quantifying interest is imprecise and not all shortcuts are worth repaying.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Refactoring
items.description: Refactoring is changing the internal structure of code to improve readability, simplicity, or design without altering its externally observable behavior. Done in small, behavior-preserving steps under test coverage, it keeps a codebase malleable and counteracts the natural accumulation of complexity. It is a continuous hygiene activity rather than a separate project, and it depends on tests to guarantee that behavior truly stays the same.
items.applicability: Ongoing maintenance, preparing code for a new feature, and reducing the cost of future change in evolving systems.
items.example: Extracting a long method into well-named helpers and removing duplication, with the existing unit tests confirming behavior is unchanged.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Without adequate test coverage, refactoring can silently change behavior; large speculative rewrites are not refactoring.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Software architecture erosion
items.description: Architecture erosion is the gradual divergence of a system's as-built structure from its intended design, as expedient changes introduce dependencies and shortcuts that violate the planned boundaries. Over time these violations accumulate, dependency cycles appear, and the system becomes harder to reason about and change. Erosion is countered by making architectural rules explicit and enforcing them, for example through dependency checks and layering constraints in the build.
items.applicability: Long-lived, multi-module systems where intended layering or boundaries must survive years of incremental change.
items.example: Enforcing that a domain Core project references no web or persistence packages so controllers cannot leak framework concerns inward, preventing cyclic dependencies.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Automated checks catch structural violations but not all conceptual drift; some architectural intent is hard to encode as a rule.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Maintainability metrics
items.description: Maintainability metrics are quantitative indicators meant to approximate how easy code is to understand and change, including cyclomatic complexity, coupling, cohesion, code churn, and composite maintainability indices. They are useful as screening signals to flag risky hotspots, but they are proxies rather than direct measures of true maintainability. Healthy use treats them as conversation starters for human review, not as targets to be optimized blindly.
items.applicability: Identifying high-risk modules, tracking trends over time, and prioritizing where to invest review or refactoring effort.
items.example: Flagging the methods with the highest complexity and change frequency as candidates for refactoring before they accumulate defects.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Metrics correlate imperfectly with real maintainability and can be gamed; optimizing a metric directly often harms the underlying goal.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Limits of defect prediction
items.description: Defect-prediction models use historical code and process metrics to estimate which files or changes are most likely to contain bugs, helping focus review and testing effort. Their accuracy is constrained by noisy and incomplete defect labels, weak transfer between projects, and the fact that the most predictive signals are often coarse change-history features. Predictions indicate elevated risk, not certainty, and act as prioritization aids rather than verdicts.
items.applicability: Allocating limited review and test resources toward the changes most likely to be defect-prone.
items.example: Routing extra scrutiny to files with high recent churn and many contributors because history suggests they are riskier, while still testing everything.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Cross-project models generalize poorly and labels are noisy; predictions guide attention but must not replace verification.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Secure-coding principles
items.description: Secure coding applies defensive practices throughout development to prevent common, well-catalogued vulnerability classes such as injection, broken access control, and insecure deserialization. Core principles include validating and encoding untrusted input, enforcing least privilege, failing securely, defense in depth, and never trusting client-supplied data for authorization. These practices are most effective when built into design and review rather than bolted on after a system is complete.
items.applicability: All application development handling untrusted input or sensitive data, particularly in regulated financial and middleware systems.
items.example: Using parameterized queries instead of string-concatenated SQL, and enforcing authorization server-side on every request rather than relying on hidden UI controls.
items.limitation: Official technical documentation. Secure-coding guidance reduces but does not eliminate risk; it is awareness only, not a security assurance or penetration-test substitute.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Process improvement
items.description: Process improvement is the structured analysis and redesign of how work flows in order to reduce waste, variation, defects, and cycle time while raising quality and predictability. Established approaches frame improvement as continuous and data-informed, focusing on bottlenecks and root causes rather than local optimizations. Sustainable gains require measuring the current state, changing the process, and verifying the effect rather than assuming the change helped.
items.applicability: Operational workflows, software delivery pipelines, and back-office processes where throughput, quality, or cost need improvement.
items.example: Mapping a manual document-import workflow, finding that an approval step is the bottleneck, and automating it, then measuring the cycle-time change.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Improvements are context-specific and can shift bottlenecks elsewhere; this is general management awareness, not financial advice.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Digital transformation
items.description: Digital transformation is the organization-wide adoption of digital technology to reshape operations, customer experience, and business models, not merely the digitization of existing manual steps. It is as much about culture, skills, and process redesign as about technology, and initiatives commonly underdeliver when they automate broken processes or neglect change management. Success is typically incremental and tied to clear business outcomes rather than technology adoption for its own sake.
items.applicability: Strategic modernization programs, legacy-system replacement, and efforts to embed data and automation into core operations.
items.example: Replacing a paper-and-spreadsheet approval chain with an integrated workflow only after redesigning the process so the digital version is genuinely simpler.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Outcomes vary widely and many programs underdeliver; this is general management awareness, not investment or strategy advice.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Workflow automation
items.description: Workflow automation replaces manual, repetitive, rules-based steps with software that executes them consistently and at scale, freeing people for judgment-intensive work and reducing handling errors. Its value depends on automating a process that is already well understood and stable; automating a poorly designed process simply produces faster mistakes. Effective automation keeps humans in the loop for exceptions and provides visibility and audit trails over what was done.
items.applicability: High-volume, repeatable operational tasks such as data import, routing, notifications, and routine reconciliations.
items.example: A background service that drains an import queue automatically, processing each file and recording an audit trail while flagging exceptions for human review.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Automating an unstable or poorly understood process amplifies its defects; exception handling and oversight remain essential.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Change management
items.description: Change management is the structured handling of the people side of organizational change so that new processes or technologies are actually adopted and sustained. It emphasizes clear rationale, stakeholder engagement, training, and reinforcement, recognizing that resistance is a predictable human response rather than a failure. Technically sound initiatives frequently fail to deliver value because adoption was treated as automatic rather than actively managed.
items.applicability: Rolling out new tools, processes, or operating models where success depends on people changing how they work.
items.example: Pairing a new internal platform launch with training, named champions, and feedback loops so staff understand and embrace the change rather than reverting to old habits.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Change frameworks are heuristics, not guarantees; effectiveness depends on context and leadership commitment.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Adoption risk
items.description: Adoption risk is the danger that a delivered capability fails to achieve its intended use and benefit because the target users do not take it up. Drivers include poor fit with real workflows, low perceived usefulness or ease of use, inadequate trust, and competing entrenched habits. Mitigating it requires designing with users, lowering friction, demonstrating value early, and monitoring actual usage rather than assuming delivery equals adoption.
items.applicability: Any new system or feature whose value depends on sustained voluntary use by employees or customers.
items.example: Piloting a new knowledge tool with a small team, measuring whether they actually use it for daily work, and removing friction before a wider rollout.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Adoption is multi-causal and context-dependent; usage metrics indicate uptake but not necessarily realized value.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Operational governance
items.description: Governance is the framework of decision rights, accountability, policies, and oversight that directs and controls how an organization or system operates. Good governance makes clear who can authorize what, establishes controls proportionate to risk, and maintains audit trails so decisions can be reviewed. Effective governance balances control with the ability to act, avoiding both unaccountable autonomy and paralyzing bureaucracy.
items.applicability: Operating models, technology platforms, and data or model lifecycles that require accountability and controlled decision-making.
items.example: Defining role-based access and an append-only audit log so every privileged action has an authorized owner and a reviewable record.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Governance design is context-specific; this is general management awareness, not legal, regulatory, or audit advice.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Operational resilience
items.description: Operational resilience is an organization's ability to continue delivering critical services through disruption and to recover quickly afterward, spanning technology, people, processes, and dependencies. It shifts focus from preventing every failure to assuming disruption will occur and ensuring important business services survive within tolerable limits. Practically it relies on mapping critical services, identifying single points of failure, testing recovery, and designing graceful degradation.
items.applicability: Critical systems and services, particularly in banking and middleware estates where outages carry significant financial and regulatory impact.
items.example: Designing a system so it still functions in a degraded mode when an optional external service is unavailable, rather than failing the whole workflow.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Resilience targets are scenario-dependent and cannot anticipate every failure; this is general awareness, not regulatory or continuity advice.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: KPI design
items.description: A key performance indicator is a measure chosen to track progress toward an objective, and its design strongly shapes behavior because people optimize what is measured. Good KPIs are tied to genuine outcomes, are hard to game, and are balanced so improving one does not silently harm another, such as pairing speed with quality. A central pitfall is that once a measure becomes a target it tends to be distorted, so KPIs need periodic review and counter-balancing measures.
items.applicability: Performance management, operational dashboards, and goal-setting where measures will drive day-to-day decisions.
items.example: Tracking import throughput alongside import error rate so that speeding up the pipeline cannot be achieved by quietly skipping problem files.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. KPIs are proxies and prone to gaming and distortion; this is general management awareness, not financial or performance advice.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Decision quality
items.description: Decision quality assesses how well a decision is made independently of how it happens to turn out, since a good process can yield a bad outcome and vice versa under uncertainty. It emphasizes framing the right problem, gathering relevant information, considering genuine alternatives, applying clear values and trade-offs, and committing to action. Common failures stem from cognitive biases, anchoring on the first option, and judging decisions only by hindsight.
items.applicability: Strategic and operational choices under uncertainty, and retrospectives that should evaluate process rather than only results.
items.example: Recording the alternatives considered and the rationale for an architecture decision so it can be judged on the reasoning available at the time, not just the outcome.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Decision frameworks reduce but do not remove bias and uncertainty; this is general management awareness, not financial advice.

items.category: AI, Coding and Business Research Notes
items.title: Project-failure patterns
items.description: Project failures recur in recognizable patterns: unclear or shifting scope, unrealistic estimates, weak stakeholder engagement, poor risk management, and continued investment in a failing effort due to sunk-cost reasoning. Many failures are visible early through warning signs that go unacted upon rather than sudden surprises. Reducing failure risk relies on clear objectives, incremental delivery with real feedback, honest status reporting, and willingness to stop or re-scope.
items.applicability: Planning, governing, and reviewing software and transformation projects, and conducting honest post-mortems.
items.example: Catching scope creep and slipping milestones early in a steering review and re-scoping to a smaller deliverable rather than pushing the original plan to a costly failure.
items.limitation: Synthesis of established concepts; topic-family attribution only, not a specific peer-reviewed citation — verification required. Failure patterns are heuristics, not deterministic predictors; this is general management awareness, not financial or assurance advice.
